sentence_reviews.text: Schemas, grammars, and formal languages represent allowable structure for JSON, SQL, code, tool calls, plans, or domain records.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Structured-output survey on constrained/structured generation; AIMA formal languages/planning/logic context.
sentence_reviews.notes: The structured-output source supports schemas/grammars as constraints for output formats. The list of JSON, SQL, code, tool calls, plans, and domain records is synthesized.

sentence_reviews.text: They give generators a machine-checkable contract instead of relying solely on prose format instructions.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Structured-output survey discussion of constrained decoding/structured generation and format compliance.
sentence_reviews.notes: The source supports machine-checkable structure and constrained generation, while 'contract' versus prose instructions is synthesized wording.

sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Structured-output survey examples of schema-constrained/JSON output generation.
sentence_reviews.notes: JSON Schema is a direct example of a schema formalism for allowable JSON structure.

sentence_reviews.evidence_location: AIMA formal language context; structured-output survey discusses grammar-constrained generation.
sentence_reviews.notes: Context-free grammars are formal grammars used to specify allowable syntax.

sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Structured-output survey/tool-call schema context; AIMA programming/type-related formalism context.
sentence_reviews.notes: Typed function signatures are a plausible formal interface constraint, though the exact phrase is not confirmed directly from the packet sources.

sentence_reviews.text: Syntactic validity does not ensure semantic correctness, safe values, authorization, or successful execution.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Structured-output survey on format/structure compliance limits; NIST AI RMF for safety/risk considerations.
sentence_reviews.notes: The distinction between syntax/format validity and semantic/safety correctness is supported conceptually, but the combined list is synthesized.

sentence_reviews.text: Schemas, grammars & formal output languages rests on the formal or computational capabilities supplied by Discrete mathematics & logic.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: AIMA formal language/logic context; structured-output survey grammar/schema constraints.
sentence_reviews.notes: Formal grammars and constraints are discrete symbolic/logical structures.

sentence_reviews.text: Schemas, grammars & formal output languages rests on the formal or computational capabilities supplied by Programming languages, compilers & type systems.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Structured-output survey on code/tool-call/output constraints; AIMA programming and formal language context.
sentence_reviews.notes: Grammars, formal languages, and typed signatures align with programming language/type-system concepts.

sentence_reviews.text: Schemas, grammars & formal output languages consumes, emits, or organizes information using Tokens, vocabularies & symbolic units.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Structured-output survey constrained decoding context; AIMA formal-language context.
sentence_reviews.notes: Formal languages and constrained decoding operate over tokens/symbols, though the exact rationale is graph-derived.

edge_reviews.notes: Schemas, grammars, constraints, and formal languages are discrete symbolic/logical constructs; requires/core is defensible.
Formal output languages, grammars, and typed tool/function interfaces are closely tied to programming-language and type-system concepts.
Grammars and constrained generation organize token/symbol sequences; represents_with/common is supported.
